question: What is the pressurizer volume according to Table 5.1-1?

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is responsible for removing heat from the reactor core and transferring it to the steam generators using natural circulation, driven by density and elevation differences. The RCS includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated valves, and interfaces with systems like the emergency core cooling system and chemical and volume control system. It also features mechanisms for removing noncondensible gases during both normal and emergency operations to maintain system integrity and safety.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section describes the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS), which uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to ensure safe cooling, maintain water chemistry, and provide uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements for each NuScale Power Module.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section outlines the testing and inspection requirements for the DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It specifies that DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, and details the procedures for valve testing, position verification, and a one-time in-situ test of the DHRS function during initial startup.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer, integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and separated by a baffle plate, regulates Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure, and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, with instrumentation providing real-time pressure and level data to plant control systems.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and manual calculations. It accounts for uncertainties in flow resistance and core bypass, ensuring low pressure losses to support natural circulation, and confirms that certain uncertainties, like SG tube plugging, do not affect primary coolant flow rate determination.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components in the NuScale reactor, specifying alloys and standards for each part to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments. It references relevant sections of the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and lists the specific alloys and material specifications for the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, heater sheaths, and bolting.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and supports key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various subsystems, and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections within the overall reactor system are briefly outlined.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) comply with relevant regulatory requirements, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria, by designing, fabricating, and testing reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Class 1 standards. It also clarifies the classification of connected piping and states that the 2013 Edition of the ASME BPVC is the code of record for the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and standards used for threaded fasteners in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines that preservice and inservice inspections and testing of pressure-retaining components are conducted according to the latest ASME BPVC Section XI requirements, as mandated by federal regulations, with inspection intervals and code editions specified in the inspection program.

edges.description: Section A describes operational features and functions of the pressurizer (PZR) within the reactor coolant system (RCS), while Section B provides a general design overview that identifies the PZR as a component of the RCS, thus Section A provides details that specify aspects of the general system described in Section B.

Section A provides a general system description mentioning the PZR (pressurizer) volume, while Section B gives a detailed design description of the pressurizer's physical configuration, both referring to the same component whose volume is specified in Table 5.1-1.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system and mentions the integral pressurizer as a component, while Section B gives a detailed description of the pressurizer's design and function, thus specifying details for the component introduced in Section A.

Section B describes the function and location of the pressurizer (PZR) volume, which is the subject of the analysis question, while Section A discusses system evaluation criteria but does not provide the specific pressurizer volume value referenced in Table 5.1-1.

Section A discusses the evaluation of system performance parameters, which are established based on the design described in Section B, where the pressurizer (PZR) is identified as part of the reactor coolant system.

Section A describes the physical components and materials of the pressurizer, while Section B explains the operational function of the pressurizer volume within the reactor coolant system, connecting the structural design to its system role.

Section A provides detailed descriptions of the pressurizer's components and materials, while Section B gives a general overview of the reactor coolant system, including the integral pressurizer, thus Section A specifies details for the general system described in Section B.

Section A describes operational procedures involving the pressurizer (PZR) water level and venting, while Section B explains the system's capability to remove noncondensible gases from the PZR volume, connecting both sections through their discussion of PZR volume management and degasification.

Section A describes operational procedures involving the pressurizer (PZR), while Section B provides a general design overview of the reactor coolant system (RCS) that includes the PZR as a component, thus Section A provides details for the general system described in Section B.

Section A describes the physical components and materials of the pressurizer, while Section B discusses operational procedures involving the pressurizer's water level and pressure, both referencing the same pressurizer system but focusing on different aspects (design vs. operation).

Section A establishes that the reactor coolant pressure boundary components, including the pressurizer, comply with regulatory codes, while Section B describes the functional role and features of the pressurizer volume within the system, connecting compliance requirements to system design.

Section A establishes that the reactor coolant boundary components, including the pressurizer, comply with regulatory codes, while Section B provides a general design description of the reactor coolant system that includes the pressurizer as a component.

Section A and Section B both discuss compliance with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code requirements for different classes of reactor coolant system components, connecting them through regulatory design and inspection standards.

Section B describes the function and features of the pressurizer (PZR) volume within the reactor coolant system, while Section A discusses the materials and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners used in components including the PZR heater bundle closures, thus connecting through the physical components associated with the PZR volume.

Section A discusses the materials and requirements for threaded fasteners in components including the pressurizer, while Section B provides a general summary of the reactor coolant system and its components, including the pressurizer, thus Section A specifies details for the general system described in Section B.

Section A and Section B both discuss inspection and testing requirements for reactor coolant system components, referencing compliance with ASME BPVC standards, thereby connecting through shared regulatory prerequisites for material and inspection standards.

Section A establishes that all pressure-retaining components, including those mentioned in Section B, must comply with ASME BPVC Section III, while Section B specifies that the threaded fasteners in these components are selected and designed in accordance with those same code requirements.
